topic: Developments in Christian Thought: Pluralism, Liberation Theology, Gender
subject: religious-studies

items.correct: The 'blowing out' of the fires of craving, hatred and delusion — liberation from the cycle of rebirth (samsara), not a heavenly afterlife.
items.misconception: Nirvana is the Buddhist version of heaven.
items.random_wrong: Nirvana is the name of a Buddhist god.
items.why: Students map nirvana onto Christian heaven — a place you go after dying. Nirvana is the cessation of the conditions that cause rebirth; it is more an end than a destination.

items.q: What is the central principle of situation ethics?
items.correct: The only intrinsically good thing is agape love; rules can be broken if doing so produces the most loving outcome in the situation.
items.misconception: You should do whatever feels right in the moment.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics says rules should always be followed.
items.why: Situation ethics is principled (love), not just intuition or relativism. Students collapse it into 'whatever feels right' — Fletcher's actual theory is more disciplined than that.

items.q: How do samsara, karma and moksha fit together in Hindu thought?
items.correct: Samsara is the cycle of rebirth; karma is the law of action and consequence that drives rebirth; moksha is liberation from samsara — these form a connected chain, not three independent ideas.
items.misconception: Samsara, karma and moksha are three separate Hindu deities.
items.random_wrong: Moksha is the Hindu word for heaven.
items.why: Students learn the terms as a vocabulary list and miss the structural relationship: karma keeps you in samsara, and moksha releases you. Without the connection, the soteriology doesn't make sense.

items.q: Does Hick's pluralism claim that all religions teach the same things?
items.correct: No — Hick acknowledged deep doctrinal differences; his claim is that the great traditions are different culturally conditioned responses to the same ultimate Real, all capable of transforming people.
items.misconception: Hick's pluralism says all religions are identical and teach exactly the same doctrines.
items.random_wrong: Hick's pluralism says all religions are completely false.
items.why: Hick's analogy is the Copernican shift: religions orbit the Real differently. Flattening this into 'all religions are the same' misses his careful distinction between the Real in itself and as experienced.

items.q: Is liberation theology essentially a programme of violent Marxist revolution?
items.correct: No — it uses elements of Marxist social analysis to diagnose structural injustice and calls for the church's 'preferential option for the poor'; the Vatican's 1984 critique targeted its borrowings, and Gutierrez's work centres on praxis, not armed struggle.
items.misconception: Liberation theology is simply Marxist revolution with a Christian label, committed to violent overthrow.
items.random_wrong: Liberation theology teaches that poverty is a blessing the poor should accept.
items.why: Analysis is not endorsement of revolution: Gutierrez frames liberation in terms of sin, salvation and solidarity. The caricature obscures the genuine debate about how far Marxist tools belong in theology.
It's Augustine. “Aquinas” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: When did Luther post the 95 Theses?
items.why: It's 1517. “1492” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many theses did Luther post?
items.why: Exact answer: 95 theses. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
